<answer>
```verilog
module alucont (
    input aluop0, aluop1, aluop2, aluop3,
    input c0, c1, c2,
    input f0, f1, f2, f3,
    output reg [3:0] gout
);

always @(*) begin
    // Combine ALU operation code and function code into 4-bit vectors
    wire [3:0] aluop = {aluop3, aluop2, aluop1, aluop0};
    wire [3:0] funct = {f3, f2, f1, f0};
    wire [2:0] ctrl = {c2, c1, c0};

    case (aluop)
        4'b0000: begin // R-type: use funct code
            case (funct)
                4'b0000: gout = 4'b0010; // ADD
                4'b0010: gout = 4'b0110; // SUB
                4'b0100: gout = 4'b0000; // AND
                4'b0101: gout = 4'b0001; // OR
                4'b1010: gout = 4'b0111; // SLT
                default: gout = 4'b0000; // Default to AND
            endcase
        end
        4'b0001: gout = 4'b0010; // ADDI (add immediate)
        4'b0010: gout = 4'b0110; // BEQ (subtract for comparison)
        4'b0011: begin // Other operations using control bits
            case (ctrl)
                3'b000: gout = 4'b0000; // ANDI (AND immediate)
                3'b001: gout = 4'b0001; // ORI (OR immediate)
                default: gout = 4'b0000;
            endcase
        end
        default: gout = 4'b0010; // Default to ADD
    endcase
end

endmodule
```
</answer>

Design a Verilog module that generates a periodic clock pulse (`clk_out`) with a configurable period. The period of the pulse is determined by the 32-bit input `maxValue`, which specifies the number of clock cycles between consecutive pulses. The module should have a synchronous reset (`rst`) that initializes the internal counter to 1 and sets `clk_out` to 0. On each rising edge of the input clock (`clk`), the counter should increment by 1. When the counter reaches `maxValue`, the output `clk_out` should be asserted high for one clock cycle, and the counter should reset to 1. This Verilog module, named `clockPulser`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|-------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `clk`       | Input     | 1     | Clock signal that triggers the counter on its rising edge                   |
| `rst`       | Input     | 1     | Synchronous reset signal that initializes the counter and clears `clk_out`  |
| `maxValue`  | Input     | 32    | 32-bit value that determines the period of the output pulse                 |
| `clk_out`   | Output    | 1     | Output signal that pulses high for one clock cycle every `maxValue` cycles  |

```verilog
module reg_file (
    input [4:0] a1,
    input [4:0] a2,
    input [4:0] a3,
    input [31:0] wd3,
    input clk,
    input res,
    input we,
    output reg [31:0] rd1,
    output reg [31:0] rd2,
    output [31:0] t1,
    output [31:0] t2,
    output [31:0] t3,
    output [31:0] t4
);

    reg [31:0] reg_array [0:31];

    // Assign test signals to monitor specific registers
    assign t1 = reg_array[9];
    assign t2 = reg_array[10];
    assign t3 = reg_array[11];
    assign t4 = reg_array[12];

    always @(posedge clk or posedge res) begin
        if (res) begin
            // Asynchronous reset: Initialize all registers to 0
            integer i;
            for (i = 0; i < 32; i = i + 1) begin
                reg_array[i] <= 32'b0;
            end
            // Reset read outputs
            rd1 <= 32'b0;
            rd2 <= 32'b0;
        end else begin
            if (we) begin
                // Write data to register a3
                reg_array[a3] <= wd3;
            end else begin
                // Read data from registers a1 and a2
                rd1 <= reg_array[a1];
                rd2 <= reg_array[a2];
            end
        end
    end

endmodule
```
</answer>

Design a half adder circuit that takes two single-bit binary inputs (`a` and `b`) and produces two outputs: `sum` (the LSB of the addition result) and `carry` (the MSB of the addition result). The `sum` should be 1 if either `a` or `b` is 1, but not both. The `carry` should be 1 only if both `a` and `b` are 1. Use an `always` block to continuously monitor changes in the inputs and update the outputs accordingly. This Verilog module, named `halfadder2`, has the interface designed as follows:  

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description                        |  
|-------------|-----------|-------|------------------------------------|  
| `a`         | Input     | 1     | First single-bit binary input      |  
| `b`         | Input     | 1     | Second single-bit binary input     |  
| `sum`       | Output    | 1     | LSB of the addition result         |  
| `carry`     | Output    | 1     | MSB of the addition result         |

r1_response.content:
<answer> ```verilog
module halfadder2(a, b, sum, carry);
    input a, b;
    output reg sum, carry;

    always @(a or b) begin
        sum = a ^ b;
        carry = a & b;
    end
endmodule
``` </answer>

localparam S1 = 2'b01;
localparam S2 = 2'b10;
localparam S3 = 2'b11;

reg [1:0] state, next_state;
reg [7:0] reg_a, reg_b;

// State transition
always @(posedge clock or posedge reset) begin
    if (reset) begin
        state <= S0;
    end else begin
        state <= next_state;
    end
end

// Next state logic

    case (state)
        S0: next_state = start ? S1 : S0;
        S1: begin

        S2: next_state = (reg_a == reg_b) ? S3 : S2;
        S3: next_state = S0;

```verilog
module gcd (
    input clock,
    input reset,
    input start,
    input [7:0] a,
    input [7:0] b,
    output reg [7:0] outp,
    output reg done
);

// State definitions
localparam S0 = 2'b00;
localparam S1 = 2'b01;
localparam S2 = 2'b10;
localparam S3 = 2'b11;

reg [1:0] state, next_state;
reg [7:0] reg_a, reg_b;

// State transition
always @(posedge clock or posedge reset) begin
    if (reset) begin
        state <= S0;
    end else begin
        state <= next_state;
    end
end

// Next state logic
always @* begin
    case (state)
        S0: next_state = start ? S1 : S0;
        S1: next_state = (reg_a == 0 || reg_b == 0) ? S3 : S2;
        S2: next_state = (reg_a == reg_b) ? S3 : S2;
        S3: next_state = S0;
        default: next_state = S0;
    endcase
end

// Register updates
always @(posedge clock or posedge reset) begin
    if (reset) begin
        reg_a <= 8'd0;
        reg_b <= 8'd0;
    end else begin
        case (state)
            S0: if (start) begin
                    reg_a <= a;
                    reg_b <= b;
                end
            S2: if (reg_a > reg_b)
                    reg_a <= reg_a - reg_b;
                else
                    reg_b <= reg_b - reg_a;
            default: ; // No changes in other states
        endcase
    end
end

// Output logic
always @(posedge clock or posedge reset) begin
    if (reset) begin
        outp <= 8'd0;
        done <= 1'b0;
    end else begin
        case (state)
            S3: begin
                done <= 1'b1;
                if (reg_a == 0)
                    outp <= reg_b;
                else if (reg_b == 0)
                    outp <= reg_a;
                else
                    outp <= reg_a; // reg_a == reg_b at this point
            end
            default: begin
                done <= 1'b0;
                outp <= 8'd0;
            end
        endcase
    end
end

endmodule
```

Design a traffic light controller for a four-way intersection with two traffic sensors (`Traffic_SensorA` and `Traffic_SensorB`). Each sensor should generate a random traffic signal (`Ta` and `Tb`) using a 5-bit Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR). The traffic light controller should use a finite state machine (FSM) with four states (`S0`, `S1`, `S2`, `S3`) to manage the traffic lights. The FSM should transition between states based on the sensor inputs, ensuring that the traffic lights change appropriately to manage traffic flow. The system should reset to state `S0` when the reset signal (`rst`) is active. The traffic light controller should have the following interface:

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|-------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `clk`       | Input     | 1     | Clock signal that triggers state transitions on rising edges.               |
| `rst`       | Input     | 1     | Active-high reset signal to initialize the system to state `S0`.            |
| `Ra`        | Output    | 1     | Red light for direction A (1 = on, 0 = off).                                |
| `Ga`        | Output    | 1     | Green light for direction A (1 = on, 0 = off).                              |
| `Ya`        | Output    | 1     | Yellow light for direction A (1 = on, 0 = off).                             |
| `Rb`        | Output    | 1     | Red light for direction B (1 = on, 0 = off).                                |
| `Gb`        | Output    | 1     | Green light for direction B (1 = on, 0 = off).                              |
| `Yb`        | Output    | 1     | Yellow light for direction B (1 = on, 0 = off).                             |

The FSM should transition as follows:
- `S0`: Green light for direction A, Red light for direction B.
- `S1`: Yellow light for direction A, Red light for direction B.
- `S2`: Green light for direction B, Red light for direction A.
- `S3`: Yellow light for direction B, Red light for direction A.

The transitions between states should be triggered by the sensor inputs (`Ta` and `Tb`).

module traffic_controller (
    input clk,
    input rst,
    output Ra,
    output Ga,
    output Ya,
    output Rb,

);

<answer>
```verilog
module alu (
    input [31:0] a,
    input [31:0] b,
    input [2:0] ALUControl,
    output reg [31:0] Result,
    output reg [3:0] ALUFlags
);

    reg [32:0] sum_temp;
    reg carry_out, overflow;

    always @(*) begin
        case(ALUControl)
            3'b000: begin // ADD
                sum_temp = {1'b0, a} + {1'b0, b};
                Result = sum_temp[31:0];
                carry_out = sum_temp[32];
                overflow = (a[31] == b[31]) && (Result[31] != a[31]);
            end
            3'b001: begin // SUB
                sum_temp = {1'b0, a} + {1'b0, ~b} + 33'd1;
                Result = sum_temp[31:0];
                carry_out = sum_temp[32];
                overflow = (a[31] != b[31]) && (Result[31] != a[31]);
            end
            3'b010: begin // AND
                Result = a & b;
                carry_out = 0;
                overflow = 0;
            end
            3'b011: begin // OR
                Result = a | b;
                carry_out = 0;
                overflow = 0;
            end
            3'b100: begin // XOR
                Result = a ^ b;
                carry_out = 0;
                overflow = 0;
            end
            default: begin
                Result = 32'b0;
                carry_out = 0;
                overflow = 0;
            end
        endcase

        // Compute flags
        ALUFlags[3] = Result[31]; // Negative (N)
        ALUFlags[2] = (Result == 32'b0); // Zero (Z)
        ALUFlags[1] = carry_out; // Carry (C)
        ALUFlags[0] = overflow; // Overflow (V)
    end

endmodule
```

Design a bus response controller that manages the response to active bus requests. The controller should transition to a response state when a request is active, the system is ready, and either an OK signal or an enable signal is high. In the response state, the controller should update the output signals to reflect the input values. If the request signal goes low, the controller should reset the output signals and return to the idle state. This Verilog module, named `drv_bus_resp`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|-------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `clk_sys`   | Input     | 1     | System clock signal                                                        |
| `ready`     | Input     | 1     | Indicates the system is ready to respond                                   |
| `ok`        | Input     | 1     | Indicates the response is OK                                               |

| `ws`        | Input     | 1     | Word or source select signal (0: select s1, 1: select s2)                  |

module SN74LS298 (
    input [3:0] s1,
    input [3:0] s2,

);

```verilog
module db_fsm_Amisha (
    input clk_amisha,
    input reset_amisha,
    input sw_amisha,
    output reg db_amisha
);

parameter N = 10000; // Adjust N as needed for debounce period

// Define states
localparam [2:0] zero     = 3'd0,
                 wait1_1  = 3'd1,
                 wait1_2  = 3'd2,
                 wait1_3  = 3'd3,
                 one      = 3'd4,
                 wait0_1  = 3'd5,
                 wait0_2  = 3'd6,
                 wait0_3  = 3'd7;

reg [2:0] current_state, next_state;
reg [31:0] counter;
reg tick;

// Counter and tick generation (tick every N clock cycles)
always @(posedge clk_amisha or posedge reset_amisha) begin
    if (reset_amisha) begin
        counter <= 0;
        tick <= 0;
    end else begin
        if (counter == N - 1) begin
            counter <= 0;
            tick <= 1;
        end else begin
            counter <= counter + 1;
            tick <= 0;
        end
    end
end

// State register update
always @(posedge clk_amisha or posedge reset_amisha) begin
    if (reset_amisha)
        current_state <= zero;
    else
        current_state <= next_state;
end

// Next state logic
always @* begin
    case (current_state)
        zero: begin
            next_state = (sw_amisha && tick) ? wait1_1 : zero;
        end
        wait1_1: begin
            if (tick)
                next_state = (sw_amisha) ? wait1_2 : zero;
            else
                next_state = wait1_1;
        end
        wait1_2: begin
            if (tick)
                next_state = (sw_amisha) ? wait1_3 : zero;
            else
                next_state = wait1_2;
        end
        wait1_3: begin
            if (tick)
                next_state = (sw_amisha) ? one : zero;
            else
                next_state = wait1_3;
        end
        one: begin
            next_state = (~sw_amisha && tick) ? wait0_1 : one;
        end
        wait0_1: begin
            if (tick)
                next_state = (sw_amisha) ? one : wait0_2;
            else
                next_state = wait0_1;
        end
        wait0_2: begin
            if (tick)
                next_state = (sw_amisha) ? one : wait0_3;
            else
                next_state = wait0_2;
        end
        wait0_3: begin
            if (tick)
                next_state = (sw_amisha) ? one : zero;
            else
                next_state = wait0_3;
        end
        default: next_state = zero;
    endcase
end

// Output logic
always @* begin
    case (current_state)
        zero:      db_amisha = 1'b0;
        wait1_1,
        wait1_2,
        wait1_3:   db_amisha = 1'b0;
        one,
        wait0_1,
        wait0_2,
        wait0_3:   db_amisha = 1'b1;
        default:   db_amisha = 1'b0;
    endcase
end

endmodule
```

<answer>
```verilog
module ps2_keyboard (
    input iCLK_50,
    inout ps2_dat,
    input ps2_clk,
    input sys_clk,
    input reset,
    input reset1,
    output reg [7:0] scandata,
    output key1_on,
    output key2_on,
    output [7:0] key1_code,
    output [7:0] key2_code
);

    // Synchronize PS/2 signals to sys_clk domain
    reg [2:0] ps2_clk_sync, ps2_dat_sync;
    wire ps2_clk_falling;
    reg [10:0] rx_shift;
    reg [3:0] bit_count;
    reg data_received;
    reg break_code;
    reg [7:0] current_data;
    
    reg key1_on_reg, key2_on_reg;
    reg [7:0] key1_code_reg, key2_code_reg;

    assign ps2_dat = 1'bz; // PS/2 data line not driven by this module

    // Synchronize ps2_clk and ps2_dat
    always @(posedge sys_clk) begin
        if (reset || reset1) begin
            ps2_clk_sync <= 3'b0;
            ps2_dat_sync <= 3'b0;
        end else begin
            ps2_clk_sync <= {ps2_clk_sync[1:0], ps2_clk};
            ps2_dat_sync <= {ps2_dat_sync[1:0], ps2_dat};
        end
    end

    assign ps2_clk_falling = (ps2_clk_sync[2:1] == 2'b10);

    // Shift register to capture PS/2 data bits
    always @(posedge sys_clk) begin
        if (reset || reset1) begin
            rx_shift <= 11'b0;
            bit_count <= 0;
            data_received <= 0;
        end else begin
            data_received <= 0;
            if (ps2_clk_falling) begin
                rx_shift <= {ps2_dat_sync[2], rx_shift[10:1]};
                bit_count <= bit_count + 1;
                if (bit_count == 10) begin
                    data_received <= 1;
                    bit_count <= 0;
                end
            end
        end
    end

    // Process received data
    always @(posedge sys_clk) begin
        if (reset || reset1) begin
            break_code <= 0;
            current_data <= 8'h00;
            scandata <= 8'h00;
        end else if (data_received) begin
            // Check start and stop bits
            if (rx_shift[10] == 1'b0 && rx_shift[0] == 1'b1) begin
                // Extract and reverse data bits (LSB first)
                current_data <= {rx_shift[2], rx_shift[3], rx_shift[4], rx_shift[5], rx_shift[6], rx_shift[7], rx_shift[8], rx_shift[9]};
                scandata <= {rx_shift[2], rx_shift[3], rx_shift[4], rx_shift[5], rx_shift[6], rx_shift[7], rx_shift[8], rx_shift[9]};
                // Handle break codes
                if (break_code) begin
                    break_code <= 0;
                end else if (current_data == 8'hF0) begin
                    break_code <= 1;
                end
            end
        end
    end

    // Key state management
    always @(posedge sys_clk) begin
        if (reset || reset1) begin
            key1_on_reg <= 1'b0;
            key2_on_reg <= 1'b0;
            key1_code_reg <= 8'h00;
            key2_code_reg <= 8'h00;
        end else if (data_received && rx_shift[10] == 1'b0 && rx_shift[0] == 1'b1) begin
            if (break_code) begin
                // Break code processing
                if (current_data == key1_code_reg) begin
                    key1_on_reg <= 1'b0;
                end else if (current_data == key2_code_reg) begin
                    key2_on_reg <= 1'b0;
                end
            end else if (current_data != 8'hF0) begin
                // Make code processing
                if (current_data == key1_code_reg) begin
                    key1_on_reg <= 1'b1;
                end else if (current_data == key2_code_reg) begin
                    key2_on_reg <= 1'b1;
                end else begin
                    if (!key1_on_reg) begin
                        key1_code_reg <= current_data;
                        key1_on_reg <= 1'b1;
                    end else if (!key2_on_reg) begin
                        key2_code_reg <= current_data;
                        key2_on_reg <= 1'b1;
                    end
                end
            end
        end
    end

    assign key1_on = key1_on_reg;
    assign key2_on = key2_on_reg;
    assign key1_code = key1_code_reg;
    assign key2_code = key2_code_reg;

endmodule
```
</answer>

You are tasked with designing a pixel generation module for a game that combines the background, pipes, and a game-over screen. The module should take the current horizontal and vertical counters, pixel data for the background, pipes, and game-over screen, as well as the coordinates of two pipes and a game-over signal. The module should output a combined pixel value that represents the appropriate image based on the current position of the counters. The module should handle the following cases:
1. If the counters are outside the visible area, output the background pixel.
2. If the game is over and the counters are within the game-over screen area, output the game-over pixel.
3. If the counters are within the area of the first or second pipe, output the pipe pixel for the pipe regions and the background pixel for the gaps between the pipes.
4. Otherwise, output the background pixel.

This Verilog module, named `pixel_gen_bg_combined0`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name         | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|---------------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `h_cnt`             | Input     | 10    | Horizontal counter indicating the current horizontal position               |
| `v_cnt`             | Input     | 10    | Vertical counter indicating the current vertical position                   |
| `background_pixel`  | Input     | 12    | Pixel data for the background                                               |
| `pipe_pixel`        | Input     | 12    | Pixel data for the pipes                                                    |
| `pipe1_x`           | Input     | 10    | X-coordinate of the first pipe                                              |
| `up_pipe1_y`        | Input     | 10    | Upper Y-coordinate of the first pipe                                        |
| `down_pipe1_y`      | Input     | 10    | Lower Y-coordinate of the first pipe                                        |
| `pipe2_x`           | Input     | 10    | X-coordinate of the second pipe                                             |
| `up_pipe2_y`        | Input     | 10    | Upper Y-coordinate of the second pipe                                       |
| `down_pipe2_y`      | Input     | 10    | Lower Y-coordinate of the second pipe                                       |
| `gameover`          | Input     | 1     | Signal indicating if the game is over                                       |
| `gameover_pixel`    | Input     | 12    | Pixel data for the game-over screen                                         |
| `combined0_bg_pixel`| Output    | 12    | Combined pixel output representing the background, pipes, or game-over screen|

    input [9:0] h_cnt,
    input [9:0] v_cnt,
    input [11:0] background_pixel,
    input [11:0] pipe_pixel,
    input [9:0] pipe1_x,
    input [9:0] up_pipe1_y,
    input [9:0] down_pipe1_y,
    input [9:0] pipe2_x,
    input [9:0] up_pipe2_y,
    input [9:0] down_pipe2_y,
    input gameover,
    input [11:0] gameover_pixel,
    output reg [11:0] combined0_bg_pixel
);

    // Parameters for visible area
    localparam VISIBLE_H_START = 0;
    localparam VISIBLE_H_END = 639; // 640 pixels
    localparam VISIBLE_V_START = 0;
    localparam VISIBLE_V_END = 479; // 480 pixels

    // Parameters for game-over screen area

    localparam GAMEOVER_H_END = 439; // 240 pixels wide
    localparam GAMEOVER_V_START = 200;
    localparam GAMEOVER_V_END = 279; // 80 pixels tall

    // Pipe parameters (width)

    // Flags for game-over screen
    wire gameover_area;
    assign gameover_area = (h_cnt >= GAMEOVER_H_START) && (h_cnt <= GAMEOVER_H_END) &&
                           (v_cnt >= GAMEOVER_V_START) && (v_cnt <= GAMEOVER_V_END);

    // Flags for pipes

    wire pipe1_upper, pipe1_lower, pipe2_upper, pipe2_lower;

```verilog
module pixel_gen_bg_combined0 (
    input [9:0] h_cnt,
    input [9:0] v_cnt,
    input [11:0] background_pixel,
    input [11:0] pipe_pixel,
    input [9:0] pipe1_x,
    input [9:0] up_pipe1_y,
    input [9:0] down_pipe1_y,
    input [9:0] pipe2_x,
    input [9:0] up_pipe2_y,
    input [9:0] down_pipe2_y,
    input gameover,
    input [11:0] gameover_pixel,
    output reg [11:0] combined0_bg_pixel
);

    // Visible area parameters (640x480)
    localparam VISIBLE_H_START = 10'd0;
    localparam VISIBLE_H_END = 10'd639;
    localparam VISIBLE_V_START = 10'd0;
    localparam VISIBLE_V_END = 10'd479;

    // Game-over screen area (centered 240x80)
    localparam GAMEOVER_H_START = 10'd200;
    localparam GAMEOVER_H_END = 10'd439;
    localparam GAMEOVER_V_START = 10'd200;
    localparam GAMEOVER_V_END = 10'd279;

    // Pipe width
    localparam PIPE_WIDTH = 10'd50;

    wire visible;
    assign visible = (h_cnt >= VISIBLE_H_START) && (h_cnt <= VISIBLE_H_END) &&
                     (v_cnt >= VISIBLE_V_START) && (v_cnt <= VISIBLE_V_END);

    wire gameover_area;
    assign gameover_area = (h_cnt >= GAMEOVER_H_START) && (h_cnt <= GAMEOVER_H_END) &&
                           (v_cnt >= GAMEOVER_V_START) && (v_cnt <= GAMEOVER_V_END);

    // Pipe 1 logic
    wire pipe1_h_active;
    wire pipe1_upper, pipe1_lower, pipe1_active, pipe1_gap;
    assign pipe1_h_active = (h_cnt >= pipe1_x) && (h_cnt < (pipe1_x + PIPE_WIDTH));
    assign pipe1_upper = (v_cnt <= up_pipe1_y);
    assign pipe1_lower = (v_cnt >= down_pipe1_y);
    assign pipe1_active = pipe1_h_active && (pipe1_upper || pipe1_lower);
    assign pipe1_gap = pipe1_h_active && !pipe1_active;

    // Pipe 2 logic
    wire pipe2_h_active;
    wire pipe2_upper, pipe2_lower, pipe2_active, pipe2_gap;
    assign pipe2_h_active = (h_cnt >= pipe2_x) && (h_cnt < (pipe2_x + PIPE_WIDTH));
    assign pipe2_upper = (v_cnt <= up_pipe2_y);
    assign pipe2_lower = (v_cnt >= down_pipe2_y);
    assign pipe2_active = pipe2_h_active && (pipe2_upper || pipe2_lower);
    assign pipe2_gap = pipe2_h_active && !pipe2_active;

    wire combined_pipe_active = pipe1_active || pipe2_active;
    wire combined_pipe_gap = pipe1_gap || pipe2_gap;

    always @* begin
        if (!visible) begin
            combined0_bg_pixel = background_pixel;
        end else if (gameover && gameover_area) begin
            combined0_bg_pixel = gameover_pixel;
        end else if (combined_pipe_active) begin
            combined0_bg_pixel = pipe_pixel;
        end else if (combined_pipe_gap) begin
            combined0_bg_pixel = background_pixel;
        end else begin
            combined0_bg_pixel = background_pixel;
        end
    end

endmodule
```
</answer>

Design a VGA pixel generator that dynamically changes the color of a specific rectangular area on the screen based on user input. The module should take in a reset signal, a clock signal, vertical and horizontal counters, a valid signal, and five key inputs. The valid signal indicates when the pixel data should be generated. The key inputs (`key_C`, `key_U`, `key_D`, `key_L`, `key_R`) should control the color of the rectangular area. The rectangular area is defined by the horizontal range 20 to 660 and the vertical range 10 to 240. When a key is pressed, the color of the area should change to a predefined value, and a counter should be used to manage the timing of the color change. The module should output the VGA color signals (`vgaRed`, `vgaGreen`, `vgaBlue`) based on the current pixel position and the selected color. This Verilog module, named `pixel_gen`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|-------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `reset`     | Input     | 1     | Reset signal to initialize the module                                       |
| `clk`       | Input     | 1     | Clock signal for synchronization                                            |
| `v_cnt`     | Input     | 10    | Vertical counter for pixel position                                         |
| `h_cnt`     | Input     | 10    | Horizontal counter for pixel position                                       |
| `valid`     | Input     | 1     | Signal indicating when pixel data should be generated                       |
| `key_C`     | Input     | 1     | Key input to change the area color to a specific value                      |
| `key_U`     | Input     | 1     | Key input to change the area color to a specific value                      |
| `key_D`     | Input     | 1     | Key input to change the area color to a specific value                      |
| `key_L`     | Input     | 1     | Key input to change the area color to a specific value                      |
| `key_R`     | Input     | 1     | Key input to change the area color to a specific value                      |
| `vgaRed`    | Output    | 4     | VGA red color signal                                                        |
| `vgaGreen`  | Output    | 4     | VGA green color signal                                                      |
| `vgaBlue`   | Output    | 4     | VGA blue color signal                                                       |

r1_response.content:
<answer>
```verilog
module pixel_gen (
    input reset,
    input clk,
    input [9:0] v_cnt,
    input [9:0] h_cnt,
    input valid,
    input key_C,
    input key_U,
    input key_D,
    input key_L,
    input key_R,
    output [3:0] vgaRed,
    output [3:0] vgaGreen,
    output [3:0] vgaBlue
);

    // Key synchronization registers
    reg key_C_sync1, key_C_sync2;
    reg key_U_sync1, key_U_sync2;
    reg key_D_sync1, key_D_sync2;
    reg key_L_sync1, key_L_sync2;
    reg key_R_sync1, key_R_sync2;

    // Edge detection registers
    reg key_C_prev, key_U_prev, key_D_prev, key_L_prev, key_R_prev;

    // Timing counter
    parameter TIMEOUT = 500000;
    reg [31:0] counter;

    // Current color registers
    reg [3:0] current_red;
    reg [3:0] current_green;
    reg [3:0] current_blue;

    // Synchronize key inputs
    always @(posedge clk or posedge reset) begin
        if (reset) begin
            key_C_sync1 <= 0;
            key_C_sync2 <= 0;
            key_U_sync1 <= 0;
            key_U_sync2 <= 0;
            key_D_sync1 <= 0;
            key_D_sync2 <= 0;
            key_L_sync1 <= 0;
            key_L_sync2 <= 0;
            key_R_sync1 <= 0;
            key_R_sync2 <= 0;
        end else begin
            key_C_sync1 <= key_C;
            key_C_sync2 <= key_C_sync1;
            key_U_sync1 <= key_U;
            key_U_sync2 <= key_U_sync1;
            key_D_sync1 <= key_D;
            key_D_sync2 <= key_D_sync1;
            key_L_sync1 <= key_L;
            key_L_sync2 <= key_L_sync1;
            key_R_sync1 <= key_R;
            key_R_sync2 <= key_R_sync1;
        end
    end

    // Edge detection logic
    always @(posedge clk or posedge reset) begin
        if (reset) begin
            key_C_prev <= 0;
            key_U_prev <= 0;
            key_D_prev <= 0;
            key_L_prev <= 0;
            key_R_prev <= 0;
        end else begin
            key_C_prev <= key_C_sync2;
            key_U_prev <= key_U_sync2;
            key_D_prev <= key_D_sync2;
            key_L_prev <= key_L_sync2;
            key_R_prev <= key_R_sync2;
        end
    end

    wire key_C_rise = ~key_C_prev && key_C_sync2;
    wire key_U_rise = ~key_U_prev && key_U_sync2;
    wire key_D_rise = ~key_D_prev && key_D_sync2;
    wire key_L_rise = ~key_L_prev && key_L_sync2;
    wire key_R_rise = ~key_R_prev && key_R_sync2;

    // Timing counter management
    always @(posedge clk or posedge reset) begin
        if (reset) begin
            counter <= 0;
        end else begin
            if (counter != 0)
                counter <= counter - 1;
            else if (key_C_rise || key_U_rise || key_D_rise || key_L_rise || key_R_rise)
                counter <= TIMEOUT;
        end
    end

    // Color selection logic
    always @(posedge clk or posedge reset) begin
        if (reset) begin
            current_red <= 0;
            current_green <= 0;
            current_blue <= 0;
        end else if (counter == 0) begin
            if (key_C_rise) begin
                current_red <= 4'hF;
                current_green <= 0;
                current_blue <= 0;
            end else if (key_U_rise) begin
                current_red <= 0;
                current_green <= 4'hF;
                current_blue <= 0;
            end else if (key_D_rise) begin
                current_red <= 0;
                current_green <= 0;
                current_blue <= 4'hF;
            end else if (key_L_rise) begin
                current_red <= 4'hF;
                current_green <= 4'hF;
                current_blue <= 0;
            end else if (key_R_rise) begin
                current_red <= 4'hF;
                current_green <= 0;
                current_blue <= 4'hF;
            end
        end
    end

    // Rectangle area check
    wire in_rect = (h_cnt >= 20) && (h_cnt <= 660) && (v_cnt >= 10) && (v_cnt <= 240);

    // Output assignment
    assign vgaRed = (valid && in_rect) ? current_red : 4'b0;
    assign vgaGreen = (valid && in_rect) ? current_green : 4'b0;
    assign vgaBlue = (valid && in_rect) ? current_blue : 4'b0;

endmodule
```
</answer>

Design a control unit for a simple processor that decodes the instruction opcode and function code to generate control signals for the ALU, register file, and memory. The control unit should handle various instruction types, including arithmetic, logical, load/store, and branch instructions. The control unit should generate signals to control the ALU operation, register write enable, memory write enable, and program counter source, among others. The control unit should also handle conditional branching based on the zero flag (`Z`). The Verilog module, named `CONUNIT`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|-------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `Op`        | Input     | 6     | Opcode of the instruction                                                   |
| `Func`      | Input     | 6     | Function code for R-type instructions                                       |
| `Z`         | Input     | 1     | Zero flag indicating if the result of the previous ALU operation was zero  |
| `Regrt`     | Output    | 1     | Signal to select the destination register                                   |
| `Se`        | Output    | 1     | Signal to sign-extend immediate values                                      |
| `Wreg`      | Output    | 1     | Signal to enable writing to the register file                              |
| `Aluqb`     | Output    | 1     | Signal to select the second ALU operand                                     |
| `Aluc`      | Output    | 4     | ALU control signals                                                         |
| `Wmem`      | Output    | 1     | Signal to enable writing to memory                                          |
| `Pcsrc`     | Output    | 2     | Signal to select the program counter source                                 |
| `Reg2reg`   | Output    | 1     | Signal to select the source for register write-back                         |
| `Aluqa`     | Output    | 1     | Signal to select the first ALU operand                                      |

r1_response.content:
<answer>
```verilog
module CONUNIT (
    input [5:0] Op,
    input [5:0] Func,
    input Z,
    output Regrt,
    output Se,
    output Wreg,
    output Aluqb,
    output [3:0] Aluc,
    output Wmem,
    output [1:0] Pcsrc,
    output Reg2reg,
    output Aluqa
);

    reg Regrt_r;
    reg Se_r;
    reg Wreg_r;
    reg Aluqb_r;
    reg [3:0] Aluc_r;
    reg Wmem_r;
    reg [1:0] Pcsrc_r;
    reg Reg2reg_r;
    reg Aluqa_r;

    always @(*) begin
        // Default values
        Regrt_r = 0;
        Se_r = 0;
        Wreg_r = 0;
        Aluqb_r = 0;
        Aluc_r = 4'b0000;
        Wmem_r = 0;
        Pcsrc_r = 2'b00;
        Reg2reg_r = 0;
        Aluqa_r = 0;

        case (Op)
            6'b000000: begin // R-type
                Regrt_r = 0;
                Wreg_r = 1;
                Aluqb_r = 0;
                Se_r = 0;
                Aluqa_r = 0;
                case (Func)
                    6'b100000: Aluc_r = 4'b0010; // add
                    6'b100010: Aluc_r = 4'b0110; // sub
                    6'b100100: Aluc_r = 4'b0000; // and
                    6'b100101: Aluc_r = 4'b0001; // or
                    6'b100110: Aluc_r = 4'b0010; // xor
                    6'b100111: Aluc_r = 4'b1100; // nor
                    6'b101010: Aluc_r = 4'b0111; // slt
                    6'b000000: begin Aluc_r = 4'b0011; Aluqa_r = 1; end // sll
                    6'b000010: begin Aluc_r = 4'b0100; Aluqa_r = 1; end // srl
                    6'b000011: begin Aluc_r = 4'b0101; Aluqa_r = 1; end // sra
                    default: Aluc_r = 4'b0000;
                endcase
            end
            6'b001000: begin // addi
                Regrt_r = 1;
                Se_r = 1;
                Wreg_r = 1;
                Aluqb_r = 1;
                Aluc_r = 4'b0010;
            end
            6'b001100: begin // andi
                Regrt_r = 1;
                Se_r = 0;
                Wreg_r = 1;
                Aluqb_r = 1;
                Aluc_r = 4'b0000;
            end
            6'b001101: begin // ori
                Regrt_r = 1;
                Se_r = 0;
                Wreg_r = 1;
                Aluqb_r = 1;
                Aluc_r = 4'b0001;
            end
            6'b001110: begin // xori
                Regrt_r = 1;
                Se_r = 0;
                Wreg_r = 1;
                Aluqb_r = 1;
                Aluc_r = 4'b0010;
            end
            6'b100011: begin // lw
                Regrt_r = 1;
                Se_r = 1;
                Wreg_r = 1;
                Aluqb_r = 1;
                Aluc_r = 4'b0010;
                Reg2reg_r = 1;
            end
            6'b101011: begin // sw
                Se_r = 1;
                Aluqb_r = 1;
                Aluc_r = 4'b0010;
                Wmem_r = 1;
            end
            6'b000100: begin // beq
                Se_r = 1;
                Aluqb_r = 0;
                Aluc_r = 4'b0110;
                Pcsrc_r = Z ? 2'b01 : 2'b00;
            end
            6'b000101: begin // bne
                Se_r = 1;
                Aluqb_r = 0;
                Aluc_r = 4'b0110;
                Pcsrc_r = (~Z) ? 2'b01 : 2'b00;
            end
            default: ; // Defaults already set
        endcase
    end

    assign Regrt = Regrt_r;
    assign Se = Se_r;
    assign Wreg = Wreg_r;
    assign Aluqb = Aluqb_r;
    assign Aluc = Aluc_r;
    assign Wmem = Wmem_r;
    assign Pcsrc = Pcsrc_r;
    assign Reg2reg = Reg2reg_r;
    assign Aluqa = Aluqa_r;

endmodule
``` 
</answer>

module e1_crc4 #(
    parameter INIT = 4'h0,
    parameter POLY = 4'h3
) (
    input clk,
    input rst,
    input in_bit,
    input in_first,
    input in_valid,
    output reg [3:0] out_crc4
);

    reg [3:0] current_crc;
    wire feedback;
    wire [3:0] shifted;
    wire [3:0] next_crc;

    assign feedback = current_crc[3] ^ in_bit;
    assign shifted = {current_crc[2:0], 1'b0};
    assign next_crc = feedback ? (shifted ^ POLY) : shifted;

    always @(posedge clk) begin
        if (rst) begin
            current_crc <= INIT;
        end else if (in_first) begin
            current_crc <= INIT;
        end else if (in_valid) begin
            current_crc <= next_crc;
        end
    end

    assign out_crc4 = current_crc;

endmodule
``` </answer>

);

    reg [7:0] shift_reg;
    reg en_prev;

            sck <= 0;
            sdat <= 0;

endmodule

```verilog
module sif (
    input clk,
    input rst,
    input en,
    input [7:0] data,
    output reg sck,
    output sdat,
    output reg sen
);

    reg [3:0] counter;
    reg [7:0] shift_reg;
    reg en_prev;

    // Combinational assignment for serial data output
    assign sdat = (sen) ? shift_reg[7] : 1'b0;

    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst) begin
        if (!rst) begin
            en_prev <= 0;
            shift_reg <= 0;
            sen <= 0;
            counter <= 0;
            sck <= 0;
        end else begin
            en_prev <= en;

            // Detect rising edge of 'en' to start transmission
            if (en && !en_prev) begin
                sen <= 1;
                shift_reg <= data;  // Load data into shift register
                counter <= 0;
            end

            if (sen) begin
                counter <= counter + 1;

                // End transmission after 16 cycles (4-bit counter)
                if (counter == 4'b1111) begin
                    sen <= 0;
                    counter <= 0;
                end

                // Generate serial clock (toggles every cycle during transmission)
                sck <= counter[0];

                // Shift data on falling edge of sck (even counter)
                if (counter[0] == 0) begin
                    shift_reg <= {shift_reg[6:0], 1'b0};
                end
            end else begin
                sck <= 0;
                counter <= 0;
            end
        end
    end

endmodule
```
</answer>

<answer>
```verilog
module ALU (
    input [31:0] ALUIn1,
    input [31:0] ALUIn2,
    input [4:0] shamt,
    input [3:0] ALUsel,
    output reg [31:0] ALUOut,
    output reg Zero
);

always @(*) begin
    case (ALUsel)
        4'b0000: ALUOut = ALUIn1 & ALUIn2;          // Bitwise AND
        4'b0001: ALUOut = ALUIn1 | ALUIn2;          // Bitwise OR
        4'b0010: ALUOut = ALUIn1 + ALUIn2;          // Addition
        4'b0011: ALUOut = ALUIn2 << shamt;          // Logical left shift (shamt)
        4'b0100: ALUOut = ALUIn2 >> shamt;          // Logical right shift (shamt)
        4'b0101: ALUOut = ALUIn2 >>> shamt;         // Arithmetic right shift (shamt)
        4'b0110: ALUOut = ALUIn1 - ALUIn2;          // Subtraction
        4'b0111: ALUOut = ($signed(ALUIn1) < $signed(ALUIn2)) ? 32'd1 : 32'd0; // Comparison (signed)
        4'b1000: ALUOut = ALUIn2 << ALUIn1;         // Logical left shift by ALUIn1
        4'b1001: ALUOut = ALUIn2 >> ALUIn1;         // Logical right shift by ALUIn1
        4'b1010: ALUOut = ALUIn2 >>> ALUIn1;        // Arithmetic right shift by ALUIn1
        4'b1011: ALUOut = ALUIn1 * ALUIn2;          // Multiplication
        default: ALUOut = 32'd0;                    // Default case
    endcase

    // Zero flag is set only for subtraction (ALUsel 0110)
    Zero = (ALUsel == 4'b0110) ? (ALUOut == 32'd0) : 1'b0;
end

endmodule
```
</answer>

Design a debug controller that manages the reception and execution of commands in a debugging system. The controller should handle a communication clock, input buffer status, and data validity signals. It should also manage the readiness to receive data and control the start and completion of recording operations. The controller should implement a finite state machine with the following states: `START_MSG`, `CMD_RECEIVED`, `OPERAND_RECEIVED`, and `EXECUTING`. In the `START_MSG` state, the controller should indicate readiness to receive data and transition to the `CMD_RECEIVED` state upon receiving valid data. In the `CMD_RECEIVED` state, the controller should check if the received command is a record command (`CMD_RECORD`) and manage the corresponding actions. This Verilog module, named `debug_controller`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name   | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|---------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `comm_clock`  | Input     | 1     | Communication clock signal that triggers state transitions                 |
| `in_empty`    | Input     | 1     | Signal indicating if the input buffer is empty                             |
| `in_ready`    | Output    | 1     | Signal indicating when the controller is ready to receive data             |
| `in_valid`    | Input     | 1     | Signal indicating if the input data is valid                               |
| `in_data`     | Input     | 8     | 8-bit data input for commands and operands                                 |
| `record_start`| Output    | 1     | Signal to start recording                                                  |
| `record_done` | Input     | 1     | Signal indicating when recording is done                                   |

The Verilog code:

module debug_controller (
    input comm_clock,
    input in_empty,
    output reg in_ready,
    input in_valid,
    input [7:0] in_data,
    output record_start,
    input record_done
);

    parameter CMD_RECORD = 8'h01; // Assuming this value; problem didn't specify.

    reg [1:0] current_state, next_state;
    parameter START_MSG = 2'b00,
              CMD_RECEIVED = 2'b01,
              OPERAND_RECEIVED = 2'b10,
              EXECUTING = 2'b11;

    reg [7:0] command_reg, operand_reg;
    reg [1:0] prev_state;

    // State register
    always @(posedge comm_clock) begin
        current_state <= next_state;
        prev_state <= current_state;
    end

    // Next state logic
    always @(*) begin
        case (current_state)
            START_MSG: begin
                if (in_valid && in_ready) begin
                    next_state = CMD_RECEIVED;
                end else begin
                    next_state = START_MSG;
                end
            end
            CMD_RECEIVED: begin
                if (command_reg == CMD_RECORD) begin
                    if (in_valid && in_ready) begin
                        next_state = OPERAND_RECEIVED;
                    end else begin
                        next_state = CMD_RECEIVED;
                    end
                end else begin
                    next_state = EXECUTING;
                end
            end
            OPERAND_RECEIVED: begin
                next_state = EXECUTING;
            end
            EXECUTING: begin
                if (command_reg == CMD_RECORD) begin
                    if (record_done) begin
                        next_state = START_MSG;
                    end else begin
                        next_state = EXECUTING;
                    end
                end else begin
                    next_state = START_MSG;
                end
            end
            default: next_state = START_MSG;
        endcase
    end

    // Data latching
    always @(posedge comm_clock) begin
        if (current_state == START_MSG && in_valid && in_ready) begin
            command_reg <= in_data;
        end
        if (current_state == CMD_RECEIVED && command_reg == CMD_RECORD && in_valid && in_ready) begin
            operand_reg <= in_data;
        end
    end

    // in_ready logic
    always @(*) begin
        case (current_state)
            START_MSG: in_ready = 1'b1;
            CMD_RECEIVED: in_ready = (command_reg == CMD_RECORD) ? 1'b1 : 1'b0;
            default: in_ready = 1'b0;
        endcase
    end

    // record_start logic
    assign record_start = (current_state == EXECUTING) && (prev_state != EXECUTING) && (command_reg == CMD_RECORD);

endmodule

module debug_controller (
    input comm_clock,
    input in_empty,
    output reg in_ready,
    input in_valid,
    input [7:0] in_data,
    output record_start,
    input record_done
);

    parameter CMD_RECORD = 8'h01; // Example value, adjust as needed

    reg [1:0] current_state, next_state;
    parameter START_MSG = 2'b00,
              CMD_RECEIVED = 2'b01,
              OPERAND_RECEIVED = 2'b10,
              EXECUTING = 2'b11;

    reg [7:0] command_reg, operand_reg;
    reg [1:0] prev_state;

    // State register
    always @(posedge comm_clock) begin
        current_state <= next_state;
        prev_state <= current_state;
    end

    // Next state logic
    always @(*) begin
        case (current_state)
            START_MSG: begin
                if (in_valid && in_ready) begin
                    next_state = CMD_RECEIVED;
                end else begin
                    next_state = START_MSG;
                end
            end
            CMD_RECEIVED: begin
                if (command_reg == CMD_RECORD) begin
                    if (in_valid && in_ready) begin
                        next_state = OPERAND_RECEIVED;
                    end else begin
                        next_state = CMD_RECEIVED;

                end else begin
                    next_state = EXECUTING;
                end
            end
            OPERAND_RECEIVED: begin
                next_state = EXECUTING;
            end
            EXECUTING: begin
                if (command_reg == CMD_RECORD) begin
                    if (record_done) begin
                        next_state = START_MSG;
                    end else begin
                        next_state = EXECUTING;
                    end
                end else begin
                    next_state = START_MSG;
                end
            end
            default: next_state = START_MSG;
        endcase
    end

    // Data latching
    always @(posedge comm_clock) begin
        if (current_state == START_MSG && in_valid && in_ready) begin
            command_reg <= in_data;
        end
        if (current_state == CMD_RECEIVED && command_reg == CMD_RECORD && in_valid && in_ready) begin
            operand_reg <= in_data;
        end
    end

    // in_ready logic
    always @(*) begin
        case (current_state)
            START_MSG: in_ready = 1'b1;
            CMD_RECEIVED: in_ready = (command_reg == CMD_RECORD) ? 1'b1 : 1'b0;
            default: in_ready = 1'b0;
        endcase
        // Gate in_ready with buffer not empty
        in_ready = in_ready & ~in_empty;
    end

    // record_start logic
    assign record_start = (current_state == EXECUTING) && (prev_state != EXECUTING) && (command_reg == CMD_RECORD);

endmodule

module debug_controller (
    input comm_clock,
    input in_empty,
    output reg in_ready,
    input in_valid,
    input [7:0] in_data,
    output record_start,
    input record_done
);

    parameter CMD_RECORD = 8'h01; // Example value, adjust as needed

    reg [1:0] current_state, next_state;
    parameter START_MSG = 2'b00,
              CMD_RECEIVED = 2'b01,
              OPERAND_RECEIVED = 2'b10,
              EXECUTING = 2'b11;

    reg [7:0] command_reg, operand_reg;
    reg [1:0] prev_state;

    // State register
    always @(posedge comm_clock) begin
        current_state <= next_state;
        prev_state <= current_state;
    end

    // Next state logic
    always @(*) begin
        case (current_state)
            START_MSG: begin
                next_state = (in_valid && in_ready) ? CMD_RECEIVED : START_MSG;
            end
            CMD_RECEIVED: begin
                if (command_reg == CMD_RECORD) begin
                    next_state = (in_valid && in_ready) ? OPERAND_RECEIVED : CMD_RECEIVED;
                end else begin
                    next_state = EXECUTING;
                end
            end
            OPERAND_RECEIVED: begin
                next_state = EXECUTING;
            end
            EXECUTING: begin
                if (command_reg == CMD_RECORD) begin
                    next_state = record_done ? START_MSG : EXECUTING;
                end else begin
                    next_state = START_MSG;
                end
            end
            default: next_state = START_MSG;
        endcase
    end

    // Data latching
    always @(posedge comm_clock) begin
        if (current_state == START_MSG && in_valid && in_ready) begin
            command_reg <= in_data;
        end
        if (current_state == CMD_RECEIVED && command_reg == CMD_RECORD && in_valid && in_ready) begin
            operand_reg <= in_data;
        end
    end

    // in_ready logic
    always @(*) begin
        case (current_state)
            START_MSG: in_ready = 1'b1;
            CMD_RECEIVED: in_ready = (command_reg == CMD_RECORD) ? 1'b1 : 1'b0;
            default: in_ready = 1'b0;
        endcase
        in_ready = in_ready & ~in_empty; // Ensure buffer is not empty
    end

    // record_start logic
    assign record_start = (current_state == EXECUTING) && (prev_state != EXECUTING) && (command_reg == CMD_RECORD);

endmodule
```
</answer>

You are tasked with designing an interface circuit that manages the flow of data between a receiver and an ALU. The circuit should have the following functionality:  
1. It should receive data in three sequential steps: first as operand A (`o_data_a`), then as operand B (`o_data_b`), and finally as the operation code (`o_operation`).  
2. It should use a 2-bit counter to track the current step in the sequence.  
3. When the counter reaches its maximum value (`2'b11`), it should reset, and a transmission start signal (`o_tx_start`) should be activated to indicate that the data is ready for processing.  
4. The circuit should output the ALU data (`o_data_out`) directly from the ALU input.  
5. The circuit should reset all outputs and the counter when the reset signal (`i_reset`) is low.  

This Verilog module, named `interface_circuit`, has the interface designed as follows:  

| Signal Name        | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|--------------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `i_clk`            | Input     | 1     | Clock signal that triggers operations on rising edges.                     |
| `i_reset`          | Input     | 1     | Active-low reset signal to initialize the circuit.                         |
| `i_rx_done_tick`   | Input     | 1     | Signal indicating that the receiver has completed data reception.          |
| `i_rx_data_in`     | Input     | `DBIT`| Received data input.                                                       |
| `i_alu_data_in`    | Input     | `DBIT`| ALU data input.                                                            |
| `o_data_a`         | Output    | `DBIT`| First operand register.                                                    |
| `o_data_b`         | Output    | `DBIT`| Second operand register.                                                   |
| `o_operation`      | Output    | `NB_OP`| Operation code register.                                                   |
| `o_tx_start`       | Output    | 1     | Signal indicating that the data is ready for transmission.                 |
| `o_data_out`       | Output    | `DBIT`| Output data from the ALU.                                                  |

r1_response.content:
<answer>
```verilog
module interface_circuit #(
    parameter DBIT = 8,
    parameter NB_OP = 4
) (
    input wire i_clk,
    input wire i_reset,
    input wire i_rx_done_tick,
    input wire [DBIT-1:0] i_rx_data_in,
    input wire [DBIT-1:0] i_alu_data_in,
    output reg [DBIT-1:0] o_data_a,
    output reg [DBIT-1:0] o_data_b,
    output reg [NB_OP-1:0] o_operation,
    output reg o_tx_start,
    output wire [DBIT-1:0] o_data_out
);

    reg [1:0] counter;

    assign o_data_out = i_alu_data_in;

    always @(posedge i_clk or negedge i_reset) begin
        if (~i_reset) begin
            counter <= 2'b00;
            o_data_a <= {DBIT{1'b0}};
            o_data_b <= {DBIT{1'b0}};
            o_operation <= {NB_OP{1'b0}};
            o_tx_start <= 1'b0;
        end else begin
            o_tx_start <= 1'b0;

            if (counter == 2'b11) begin
                counter <= 2'b00;
                o_tx_start <= 1'b1;
            end else if (i_rx_done_tick) begin
                case (counter)
                    2'b00: o_data_a <= i_rx_data_in;
                    2'b01: o_data_b <= i_rx_data_in;
                    2'b10: o_operation <= i_rx_data_in;
                    default: ;
                endcase
                counter <= counter + 1;
            end
        end
    end

endmodule
```
</answer>

Design a data flow control module that manages the transfer of 36-bit data between a source and a destination. The module should handle the following control signals: `shutoff` to stop data flow, `clear` to reset the internal state, and `reset` to initialize the module. The module should also check the readiness of both the source (`src_rdy_i`) and destination (`dst_rdy_i`) before allowing data transfer. The 34th bit of the input data (`data_i[33]`) should determine the internal `active` state. When the `shutoff` signal is active, the module should halt data flow by setting `src_rdy_o` to 0 and `dst_rdy_o` to 1. The module should update its internal state on the rising edge of the clock and ensure that the output data (`data_o`) is always the same as the input data (`data_i`). This Verilog module, named `valve36`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|-------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `clk`       | Input     | 1     | Clock signal for state updates                                             |
| `reset`     | Input     | 1     | Active-high reset signal to initialize the module                          |
| `clear`     | Input     | 1     | Active-high clear signal to reset the internal state                       |
| `shutoff`   | Input     | 1     | Signal to halt data flow                                                   |
| `data_i`    | Input     | 36    | 36-bit input data                                                          |
| `src_rdy_i` | Input     | 1     | Source readiness signal                                                    |
| `dst_rdy_i` | Input     | 1     | Destination readiness signal                                               |
| `data_o`    | Output    | 36    | 36-bit output data, always equal to `data_i`                               |
| `src_rdy_o` | Output    | 1     | Output source readiness signal, controlled by `shutoff`                    |
| `dst_rdy_o` | Output    | 1     | Output destination readiness signal, controlled by `shutoff`               |

module valve36 (

    input clear,
    input shutoff,
    input [35:0] data_i,

    input dst_rdy_i,
    output [35:0] data_o,
    output src_rdy_o,

);

    assign dst_rdy_o = shutoff ? 1'b1 : (src_rdy_i & ~shutoff);

            active <= 1'b0;
        end else if (~shutoff && src_rdy_i && dst_rdy_i) begin
            active <= data_i[33];

endmodule

Design a digital circuit that processes 8-bit input data and displays the results on LEDs and 7-segment displays based on the selected operation. The circuit should have the following functionality:
1. Perform a 4-bit addition of the upper nibble of the input data with the constant `0001` and display the result on the LEDs.
2. Perform a 4-bit addition of the upper and lower nibbles of the input data and display the result on the LEDs.
3. Perform an 8-bit addition of the upper and lower nibbles of the input data and display the result on the LEDs.
4. Perform a bitwise OR and XOR operation on the upper and lower nibbles of the input data and display the result on the LEDs.
5. Perform a bitwise OR operation on all bits of the input data and display the result on the LEDs.
6. Directly display the input data on the LEDs.
The circuit should also display the lower nibble of the input data on `HEX0`, the upper nibble on `HEX2`, and the lower and upper nibbles of the result on `HEX4` and `HEX5`, respectively. The circuit should use a 3-bit key input to select the operation and should reset the display to `00000000` for any invalid key input. This Verilog module, named `lab3part3`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|-------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `SW`        | Input     | 8     | 8-bit input data for processing                                             |
| `KEY`       | Input     | 3     | 3-bit key input to select the operation                                     |
| `LEDR`      | Output    | 8     | 8-bit output to display the result of the selected operation                |
| `HEX0`      | Output    | 7     | 7-segment display to show the lower nibble of the input data                |
| `HEX1`      | Output    | 7     | 7-segment display (unused in this design)                                   |
| `HEX2`      | Output    | 7     | 7-segment display to show the upper nibble of the input data                |
| `HEX3`      | Output    | 7     | 7-segment display (unused in this design)                                   |
| `HEX4`      | Output    | 7     | 7-segment display to show the lower nibble of the result                    |
| `HEX5`      | Output    | 7     | 7-segment display to show the upper nibble of the result                    |

r1_response.content:
<answer>
```verilog
module lab3part3(SW, KEY, LEDR, HEX0, HEX1, HEX2, HEX3, HEX4, HEX5);
    input [7:0] SW;
    input [2:0] KEY;
    output [7:0] LEDR;
    output [6:0] HEX0, HEX1, HEX2, HEX3, HEX4, HEX5;

    wire [3:0] upper = SW[7:4];
    wire [3:0] lower = SW[3:0];
    reg [7:0] result;

    // Determine result based on KEY input
    always @(*) begin
        case (KEY)
            3'd0: result = {4'b0, upper + 4'd1};               // Op1: upper + 1
            3'd1: result = {4'b0, upper + lower};              // Op2: upper + lower (4b)
            3'd2: result = (upper << 4) + lower;               // Op3: (upper<<4) + lower
            3'd3: result = {(upper | lower), (upper ^ lower)}; // Op4: OR & XOR nibbles
            3'd4: result = (|SW) ? 8'hFF : 8'h00;              // Op5: OR all bits
            3'd5: result = SW;                                 // Op6: direct display
            default: result = 8'h00;                           // Invalid keys
        endcase
    end

    assign LEDR = result;

    // 7-segment display helper function
    function [6:0] seg7;
        input [3:0] num;
        begin
            case (num)
                4'h0: seg7 = 7'b1000000;
                4'h1: seg7 = 7'b1111001;
                4'h2: seg7 = 7'b0100100;
                4'h3: seg7 = 7'b0110000;
                4'h4: seg7 = 7'b0011001;
                4'h5: seg7 = 7'b0010010;
                4'h6: seg7 = 7'b0000010;
                4'h7: seg7 = 7'b1111000;
                4'h8: seg7 = 7'b0000000;
                4'h9: seg7 = 7'b0010000;
                4'hA: seg7 = 7'b0001000;
                4'hB: seg7 = 7'b0000011;
                4'hC: seg7 = 7'b1000110;
                4'hD: seg7 = 7'b0100001;
                4'hE: seg7 = 7'b0000110;
                4'hF: seg7 = 7'b0001110;
                default: seg7 = 7'b1111111;
            endcase
        end
    endfunction

    // Connect HEX displays
    assign HEX0 = seg7(lower);
    assign HEX2 = seg7(upper);
    assign HEX4 = seg7(result[3:0]);
    assign HEX5 = seg7(result[7:4]);
    assign HEX1 = 7'b1111111;
    assign HEX3 = 7'b1111111;

endmodule
```
</answer>

The module should have a clock input (clk), a serial data input (datain), and a valid input signal (validIn) that indicates when the serial data is valid. The module should produce an 8-bit parallel output (dataout) and a valid output signal (validOut) that indicates when the parallel data is ready. The deserialization process should shift the serial data into an 8-bit temporary register when validIn is high, and the validOut signal should be set to low during this process. When validIn is low, validOut should be set to high, indicating that the deserialized data is ready. The dataout register should be updated with the contents of the temporary register when validOut is high and reset to zero when validOut is low. This Verilog module, named `deserializer`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |

| `clk`       | Input     | 1     | Clock signal that triggers the deserialization process on its rising edge  |

| `dataout`   | Output    | 8     | 8-bit parallel output of the deserialized data                             |

Design a Program Counter (PC) module for a processor that supports loading a new address, incrementing the current address by 4 (to account for 4-byte memory words), and resetting to zero. The module should have a clock input, a reset input, a load input, an increment input, a 32-bit input for the new address, and a 32-bit output for the current address. The module should update the program counter on the rising edge of the clock or reset. When reset is active, the program counter should be set to zero. When the load signal is active, the program counter should be updated with the input address. When the increment signal is active, the program counter should be incremented by 4. If neither load nor increment is active, the program counter should retain its current value. This Verilog module, named `PC`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description                                                                 |
|-------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `clk`       | Input     | 1     | Clock signal that triggers updates on the rising edge                      |
| `rst`       | Input     | 1     | Reset signal that sets the program counter to zero when active             |
| `ld`        | Input     | 1     | Load signal that updates the program counter with PC_In when active        |
| `inc`       | Input     | 1     | Increment signal that increases the program counter by 4 when active       |
| `PC_In`     | Input     | 32    | 32-bit input value to load into the program counter                        |
| `PC_Out`    | Output    | 32    | 32-bit output value representing the current program counter address       |

1. The `push` signal should be set to 0 when the state is `4'b0001`; otherwise, it should be set to 1.
2. The `pop` signals (`pop0`, `pop1`, `pop2`, `pop3`) should be set to 0 if the state is `4'b0001` or if any of the `almost_full` signals (`almost_full0`, `almost_full1`, `almost_full2`, `almost_full3`) are high. If none of the `almost_full` signals are high, the module should check the `empty_naranja` signals in a priority order to determine which FIFO should be popped.
3. The `empties` output should encapsulate the `empty` signals (`empty0_naranja`, `empty1_naranja`, `empty2_naranja`, `empty3_naranja`, `empty0_morado`, `empty1_morado`, `empty2_morado`, `empty3_morado`) into an 8-bit vector. If the state is `4'b0001`, `empties` should be set to `8'b00000000`; otherwise, it should reflect the current state of the `empty` signals.

This Verilog module, named `arbitro`, has the interface designed as follows:

|------------------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `clk`            | Input     | 1     | Clock signal that triggers the module's logic on rising edges.             |

| `empty2_naranja` | Input     | 1     | Empty signal for FIFO 2 (naranja).                                         |
| `empty3_naranja` | Input     | 1     | Empty signal for FIFO 3 (naranja).                                         |
| `empty0_morado`  | Input     | 1     | Empty signal for FIFO 0 (morado).                                          |
| `empty1_morado`  | Input     | 1     | Empty signal for FIFO 1 (morado).                                          |
| `empty2_morado`  | Input     | 1     | Empty signal for FIFO 2 (morado).                                          |
| `empty3_morado`  | Input     | 1     | Empty signal for FIFO 3 (morado).                                          |
| `push`           | Output    | 1     | Push signal for the FIFOs.                                                 |
| `pop0`           | Output    | 1     | Pop signal for FIFO 0.                                                     |
| `pop1`           | Output    | 1     | Pop signal for FIFO 1.                                                     |
| `pop2`           | Output    | 1     | Pop signal for FIFO 2.                                                     |
| `pop3`           | Output    | 1     | Pop signal for FIFO 3.                                                     |
| `empties`        | Output    | 8     | 8-bit vector encapsulating the empty signals of all FIFOs.                 |

```verilog
module arbitro (
    input clk,
    input almost_full0,
    input almost_full1,
    input almost_full2,
    input almost_full3,
    input [3:0] state,
    input empty0_naranja,
    input empty1_naranja,
    input empty2_naranja,
    input empty3_naranja,
    input empty0_morado,
    input empty1_morado,
    input empty2_morado,
    input empty3_morado,
    output reg push,
    output reg pop0,
    output reg pop1,
    output reg pop2,
    output reg pop3,
    output reg [7:0] empties
);

wire any_almost_full = almost_full0 | almost_full1 | almost_full2 | almost_full3;
wire pop_enable = (state != 4'b0001) && !any_almost_full;

wire next_push = (state == 4'b0001) ? 1'b0 : 1'b1;

wire next_pop0 = pop_enable && !empty0_naranja;
wire next_pop1 = pop_enable && empty0_naranja && !empty1_naranja;
wire next_pop2 = pop_enable && empty0_naranja && empty1_naranja && !empty2_naranja;
wire next_pop3 = pop_enable && empty0_naranja && empty1_naranja && empty2_naranja && !empty3_naranja;

wire [7:0] next_empties = (state == 4'b0001) ? 8'b0 : {
    empty0_naranja, empty1_naranja, empty2_naranja, empty3_naranja,
    empty0_morado, empty1_morado, empty2_morado, empty3_morado
};

always @(posedge clk) begin
    push <= next_push;
    pop0 <= next_pop0;
    pop1 <= next_pop1;
    pop2 <= next_pop2;
    pop3 <= next_pop3;
    empties <= next_empties;
end

endmodule
```
</answer>

| `stall_o`   | Output    | 1     | Stall signal; always set to 0 to indicate no stall condition               |

            mem[addr2] <= data_i[15:8];
            mem[addr3] <= data_i[7:0];
        end
    end

```

<answer>
```verilog
module dacpwm #(
    parameter C_pcm_bits = 12,
    parameter C_dac_bits = 4
) (
    input wire clk,
    input wire signed [C_pcm_bits-1:0] pcm,
    output reg [C_dac_bits-1:0] dac
);

localparam L_bits = C_pcm_bits - C_dac_bits;

// Split into upper (signed) and lower (unsigned) parts
wire signed [C_dac_bits-1:0] upper_signed = pcm[C_pcm_bits-1 -: C_dac_bits];
wire [L_bits-1:0] lower = pcm[L_bits-1:0];

// Convert upper to unsigned by adding offset
wire [C_dac_bits-1:0] d1 = upper_signed + (2 ** (C_dac_bits - 1));
wire [C_dac_bits-1:0] d2 = d1 + 1; // Allow wrap-around

// PWM counter
reg [L_bits-1:0] pwm_counter = 0;

always @(posedge clk) begin
    pwm_counter <= pwm_counter + 1;
end

// Select DAC value based on PWM counter and lower bits
always @(posedge clk) begin
    if (pwm_counter < lower)
        dac <= d2;
    else
        dac <= d1;
end

endmodule
```
</answer>

```verilog
module Final (
    input clk,
    input rst_n,
    input [19:0] in_data,
    output fin,
    output [2:0] opcode,
    output [19:0] i,
    output [19:0] j,
    output [19:0] out_data
);

localparam [2:0]
    INIT    = 3'b000,
    GET_N   = 3'b001,
    READ_Y  = 3'b010,
    READ_A  = 3'b011,
    READ_X  = 3'b100,
    MUL_SUB = 3'b101,
    WRITE_X = 3'b110,
    FINISH  = 3'b111;

reg [2:0] state_reg, state_next;
reg [19:0] N_reg;
reg [19:0] Y_reg, A_reg, X_reg;
reg [19:0] product_reg, sub_result_reg;
reg [19:0] i_reg;

// State register and data processing
always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
    if (!rst_n) begin
        state_reg <= INIT;
        N_reg <= 20'd0;
        Y_reg <= 20'd0;
        A_reg <= 20'd0;
        X_reg <= 20'd0;
        product_reg <= 20'd0;
        sub_result_reg <= 20'd0;
        i_reg <= 20'd0;
    end else begin
        state_reg <= state_next;
        case (state_reg)
            GET_N: N_reg <= in_data;
            READ_Y: Y_reg <= in_data;
            READ_A: A_reg <= in_data;
            READ_X: X_reg <= in_data;
            MUL_SUB: begin
                product_reg <= A_reg * X_reg;
                sub_result_reg <= Y_reg - product_reg;
            end
            WRITE_X: i_reg <= i_reg + 1;
            default: begin end
        endcase
    end
end

// Next state logic
always @* begin
    state_next = state_reg;
    case (state_reg)
        INIT: state_next = GET_N;
        GET_N: state_next = READ_Y;
        READ_Y: state_next = READ_A;
        READ_A: state_next = READ_X;
        READ_X: state_next = MUL_SUB;
        MUL_SUB: state_next = WRITE_X;
        WRITE_X: state_next = (i_reg < N_reg) ? READ_Y : FINISH;
        FINISH: state_next = FINISH;
        default: state_next = INIT;
    endcase
end

// Output assignments
assign opcode = state_reg;
assign fin = (state_reg == FINISH);
assign i = i_reg;
assign j = i_reg;
assign out_data = (state_reg == WRITE_X) ? sub_result_reg : 20'd0;

endmodule

    output reg [DATA_WIDTH-1:0] register_output1,
    output reg [DATA_WIDTH-1:0] register_output2
);

    // Define the register array
    reg [DATA_WIDTH-1:0] registers [0:(2**REGISTER_ID_WIDTH)-1];
    integer i;

    // Sequential logic for writes and reset
    always @(posedge clk_i) begin
        if (rst_i) begin
            // Reset all registers to zero
            for (i = 0; i < (2**REGISTER_ID_WIDTH); i = i + 1)
                registers[i] <= {DATA_WIDTH{1'b0}};
        end else begin
            // Priority-based write: ALU > Memory > Direct input
            if (alu_active_i)
                registers[choose_register_i1] <= alu_result_i;
            else if (mem_read_rdy_i)
                registers[choose_register_i1] <= load_value_i;
            else if (register_active_i)
                registers[choose_register_i1] <= register_input;
        end
    end

    // Combinational read logic for selected registers
    always @* begin
        register_output1 = registers[choose_register_i1];
        register_output2 = registers[choose_register_i2];
    end

endmodule
```

Design a Verilog module that generates a 7-bit output signal `clk_wait` based on a mode selection and two selection inputs. The module should operate on the rising edge of a 50 MHz clock signal (`CLK_50`). The `measure_mode` input determines the mode of operation: when `measure_mode` is 0, the module should use the `F_sel` input to select one of four predefined 7-bit patterns for `clk_wait`. When `measure_mode` is 1, the module should use the `T_sel` input to select one of three predefined 7-bit patterns for `clk_wait`. The module should ensure that `clk_wait` is updated synchronously with the clock signal. This Verilog module, named `Clock_nRST`, has the interface designed as follows:

| Signal Name     | Direction | Width | Description                                                                                      |
|-----------------|-----------|-------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `clk_wait`      | Output    | 7     | 7-bit output signal generated based on the selected mode and input values                        |
| `CLK_50`        | Input     | 1     | 50 MHz clock signal that triggers the module on its rising edge                                 |
| `F_sel`         | Input     | 2     | Selection input used when `measure_mode` is 0 to choose one of four predefined patterns          |
| `T_sel`         | Input     | 2     | Selection input used when `measure_mode` is 1 to choose one of three predefined patterns         |
| `measure_mode`  | Input     | 1     | Mode selection input: 0 for `F_sel` mode, 1 for `T_sel` mode                                     |

r1_response.content:
<answer>
```verilog
module Clock_nRST(
    output reg [6:0] clk_wait,
    input CLK_50,
    input [1:0] F_sel,
    input [1:0] T_sel,
    input measure_mode
);

    // Predefined patterns for F_sel mode (measure_mode = 0)
    localparam [6:0] F0 = 7'b0000000;
    localparam [6:0] F1 = 7'b0000001;
    localparam [6:0] F2 = 7'b0000011;
    localparam [6:0] F3 = 7'b0000111;

    // Predefined patterns for T_sel mode (measure_mode = 1)
    localparam [6:0] T0 = 7'b0001110;
    localparam [6:0] T1 = 7'b0011100;
    localparam [6:0] T2 = 7'b0111000;

    always @(posedge CLK_50) begin
        if (!measure_mode) begin
            // Select F_sel pattern
            case (F_sel)
                2'b00: clk_wait <= F0;
                2'b01: clk_wait <= F1;
                2'b10: clk_wait <= F2;
                2'b11: clk_wait <= F3;
                default: clk_wait <= F0;
            endcase
        end else begin
            // Select T_sel pattern
            case (T_sel)
                2'b00: clk_wait <= T0;
                2'b01: clk_wait <= T1;
                2'b10: clk_wait <= T2;
                default: clk_wait <= T2; // Handles T_sel = 2'b11
            endcase
        end
    end

endmodule
```
</answer>

```verilog
module part6(SW, HEX0, HEX1, HEX2, HEX3, HEX4, HEX5, HEX6, HEX7);
    input [17:0] SW;
    output [6:0] HEX0, HEX1, HEX2, HEX3, HEX4, HEX5, HEX6, HEX7;

    wire [6:0] HEX [7:0];
    assign HEX0 = HEX[0];
    assign HEX1 = HEX[1];
    assign HEX2 = HEX[2];
    assign HEX3 = HEX[3];
    assign HEX4 = HEX[4];
    assign HEX5 = HEX[5];
    assign HEX6 = HEX[6];
    assign HEX7 = HEX[7];

    genvar i;
    generate
        for (i = 0; i < 8; i = i + 1) begin : display_gen
            reg [2:0] selected_group;
            wire [3:0] sum = SW[17:15] + i;
            wire [2:0] group_sel = sum % 5;
            always @(*) begin
                case (group_sel)
                    3'd0: selected_group = SW[2:0];
                    3'd1: selected_group = SW[5:3];
                    3'd2: selected_group = SW[8:6];
                    3'd3: selected_group = SW[11:9];
                    3'd4: selected_group = SW[14:12];
                    default: selected_group = 3'b000;
                endcase
            end
            seg7_decoder decoder(.in(selected_group), .out(HEX[i]));
        end
    endgenerate
endmodule

module seg7_decoder(input [2:0] in, output reg [6:0] out);
    always @(*) begin
        case (in)
            3'b000: out = 7'b1000000; // 0
            3'b001: out = 7'b1111001; // 1
            3'b010: out = 7'b0100100; // 2
            3'b011: out = 7'b0110000; // 3
            3'b100: out = 7'b0011001; // 4
            3'b101: out = 7'b0010010; // 5
            3'b110: out = 7'b0000010; // 6
            3'b111: out = 7'b1111000; // 7
            default: out = 7'b1111111;
        endcase
    end
endmodule
```
</answer>
